title: Congenital Adrenal Hyperplasia
oneLiner: Autosomal recessive defect in cortisol synthesis (most often 21-hydroxylase deficiency) causing adrenal insufficiency, virilisation, and salt-wasting; requires lifelong replacement and stress-dosing.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Adrenal crisis (salt-wasting crisis):** hypovolaemic shock + hyponatraemia + hyperkalaemia + hypoglycaemia. *Single finding:* hypotension that does not correct with fluids alone, plus rapid drop in sodium.
- **Severe hyperkalaemia (K+ >6.5 mmol/L):** broad QRS, peaked T waves → cardiac arrest. *Single finding:* ECG showing hyperkalaemia in a shocked neonate or known CAH patient → treat empirically.
- **Missed stress-dose in sick patient:** any infection, surgery, or trauma in a known CAH patient → can precipitate crisis within hours. *Single finding:* known CAH + intercurrent illness + no extra steroid → treat immediately.
sections.Presentation:
- **Neonate (classic salt-wasting):** vomiting, lethargy, poor feeding, dehydration, ambiguous genitalia in females (46,XX), hyperpigmentation (nipples, scrotum). Hyponatraemia, hyperkalaemia, hypoglycaemia.
- **Simple virilising (no salt-wasting):** girls with ambiguous genitalia, boys with phallic enlargement, early pubic hair, accelerated growth.
- **Non-classic (late-onset):** hirsutism, oligomenorrhoea, infertility, acne, precocious puberty in children.
- **Older known CAH (adult):** admitted for surgery, infection, or trauma → missing stress-dose → crisis. Or incidentally found electrolytes.
- **Key exam findings:** look for hyperpigmentation (hands, oral mucosa), signs of virilisation (clitoromegaly, penile growth, early pubic hair), hypotension.
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **In neonate with ambiguous genitalia:** true hermaphroditism, partial androgen insensitivity syndrome, 11β-hydroxylase deficiency. *Differentiator:* 17-OHP markedly elevated only in 21-OHD.
- **In older child or adult with virilisation:** polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS), adrenal/ovarian tumour (androgen-secreting). *Differentiator:* 17-OHP + ACTH stimulation test — PCOS does not have raised 17-OHP after ACTH.
- **In adrenal crisis:** septic shock, hypovolaemia from gastroenteritis, other causes of hyponatraemia (SIADH, adrenal insufficiency not CAH), hyperkalaemia (renal failure, hypoaldosteronism).
- **Splitter:** **17-hydroxyprogesterone** — in 21-OHD it is grossly elevated; in all other adrenal insufficiency or sepsis it is normal. Do not rely on random cortisol alone.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:**
  - Capillary blood glucose (hypoglycaemia common in crisis)
  - ECG for hyperkalaemia (peaked T, widened QRS)
  - Urine output (oliguria in shock)
- **Bloods:**
  - U&Es (hyponatraemia, hyperkalaemia), glucose
  - Cortisol (random, but **do not wait**: treat first) — low <100 nmol/L suggests insufficiency
  - ACTH (elevated in primary adrenal insufficiency)
  - **17-hydroxyprogesterone (17-OHP)** — diagnostic for 21-OHD (↑↑)
  - Renin (elevated in salt-wasting form)
  - Blood gas (metabolic acidosis in crisis)
- **Imaging:**
  - **Neonate:** pelvic ultrasound for uterus/ovaries (to assign sex of rearing)
  - **Adrenal ultrasound:** may show enlarged adrenals
  - **Knee or wrist X-ray:** bone age to assess virilisation effect (advanced)
- **Special tests:**
  - **ACTH (Synacthen) stimulation test:** baseline + 30/60-min cortisol and 17-OHP (if diagnosis uncertain). In CAH, cortisol fails to rise; 17-OHP rises exaggerated.
  - Continuous glucose monitoring if hypoglycaemic episodes.
- **Never-miss test:** **17-hydroxyprogesterone** (send urgently if suspected). Also **pre-treatment cortisol** before giving hydrocortisone (but treat immediately if unstable).
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - **Adrenal crisis:** IV access, fluid bolus 20 mL/kg 0.9% saline (repeat if needed), IV hydrocortisone 100 mg (adults) or 50 mg/m² (children) immediately. Correct hypoglycaemia (10% dextrose) if present. Monitor K+ closely — treat hyperkalaemia if ECG changes.
  - **Known CAH + illness:** give "stress dose" hydrocortisone IV (or IM if no access) within 30 minutes of presentation. Do not wait for tests.
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - Continue IV hydrocortisone [check local policy] — typically 50–100 mg 6-hourly or continuous infusion 200 mg/24h (adults). Convert to oral when tolerating oral intake.
  - Start fludrocortisone for salt-wasting (adults: 50–200 mcg daily; children: dose per local protocol).
  - Correct electrolytes — sodium replacement if hyponatraemic, treat hyperkalaemia.
  - Endocrine referral for all new diagnoses.
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - **Maintenance:** oral hydrocortisone (10–20 mg/m²/day in 3 divided doses, typical adult 15–25 mg/day), fludrocortisone (50–200 mcg daily). Monitor growth in children, electrolytes, renin, bone age.
  - **Sick day rules:** double/triple oral hydrocortisone during febrile illness, vomiting, diarrhoea; if unable to take oral → IM/IV hydrocortisone. Provide steroid alert card and MedicAlert.
  - Annual endocrine review in adults (adrenal rest tumours testicular US in men).
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - **IV fluids:** 0.9% saline at maintenance rate, plus replacement for dehydration.
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** standard based on age/mobility — consider increased risk with prolonged illness.
  - **Diet:** normal as tolerated; if NBM → IV hydrocortisone + glucose.
  - **Observations:** NEWS2 4-hourly, hourly if unstable. Capillary glucose pre-meals if hypoglycaemia risk.
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line (crisis):** Hydrocortisone 100 mg IV (adults) / 50 mg/m² IV (children) stat, then 50–100 mg IV 6-hourly or 200 mg/24h continuous infusion. [Check local policy for paediatric dosing.]
- **Maintenance oral:** Hydrocortisone 10–20 mg/m²/day in 3 divided doses (e.g., morning 10 mg, afternoon 5 mg, evening 5 mg for adult). Adjust per clinical response and 17-OHP levels.
- **Mineralocorticoid:** Fludrocortisone 50–200 mcg once daily (adults). Dose adjusted by renin and electrolytes.
- **Common alternative:** Prednisolone (once daily) in adults for poor compliance — dose equivalent to hydrocortisone: prednisolone 5 mg ≈ hydrocortisone 20 mg. Use only under specialist supervision.
- **Key contraindications/interactions:**
  - Hydrocortisone: avoid live vaccines during high-dose therapy. Interacts with insulin and antidiabetics (↑ glucose).
  - Fludrocortisone: caution in hypertension, heart failure.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** No specific dose adjustment needed; monitor fluid balance in renal impairment.
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Omitting stress-dose during illness/surgery** – this is the commonest cause of crisis death. Also: giving oral hydrocortisone to a vomiting patient without parenteral replacement.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Adrenal crisis (shock, hypoglycaemia, hyperkalaemia – cardiac arrest)
  - Hyperkalaemia-related arrhythmia
  - Hypoglycaemic seizure
- **Chronic:**
  - Short stature (overtreatment with glucocorticoids suppresses growth)
  - Obesity, osteoporosis, diabetes, hypertension (iatrogenic Cushing's from over-replacement)
  - Testicular adrenal rest tumours (TART) in males → infertility, testicular pain
  - Female virilisation / menstrual disturbance / subfertility
  - Osteoporosis (prolonged steroid use)
sections.Prognosis: - Excellent with lifelong therapy and adherence to sick day rules. Salt-wasting crisis is preventable. Mortality in childhood from crisis is <5% in developed countries. Adult life expectancy normal if good control. TART can cause infertility but is treatable.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Give hydrocortisone first, ask questions later** — any patient with known adrenal insufficiency and illness needs stress-dose. Write a "steroid stress prescription" when they are admitted.
- **Always provide a written sick day rule plan and alert jewellery** for every CAH patient at discharge.
- **In a neonate with ambiguous genitalia and electrolytes derangement, start hydrocortisone + fludrocortisone while awaiting 17-OHP** — do not delay treatment to confirm diagnosis.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Giving glucocorticoids without mineralocorticoid in salt-wasting form** — will not correct sodium; may cause fluid overload.
- **Using a random cortisol to rule out CAH** — a normal cortisol does not exclude 21-OH deficiency; you need 17-OHP.
- **Assuming an adult with known CAH is "stable" on their usual dose during intercurrent illness** — they need stress dose; never leave them on maintenance only.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:**
  - Vomiting, diarrhoea, or unable to take oral steroids >1 dose
  - Fever >38°C not controlled with antipyretics
  - Severe headache, dizziness, syncope, abdominal pain, or confusion
- **Follow-up:**
  - Endocrine clinic within 1 week after a crisis. Ensure they have a steroid card, Glucagon pen (if hypoglycaemia risk), and written sick day rules.
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior / ICU if:**
  - NEWS2 ≥5 or persistent hypotension after two fluid boluses
  - K+ >6.5 mmol/L with ECG changes (broad QRS, loss of P wave)
  - GCS <13 or seizures (likely hypoglycaemia) or refractory hypoglycaemia
  - Lactate >2 mmol/L and rising
  - Any new arrhythmia or respiratory arrest
- **Immediate crash call:** cardiac arrest, suspicion of life-threatening hyperkalaemia, or seizure.

rawMarkdown:
## Congenital Adrenal Hyperplasia
**One-liner:** Autosomal recessive defect in cortisol synthesis (most often 21-hydroxylase deficiency) causing adrenal insufficiency, virilisation, and salt-wasting; requires lifelong replacement and stress-dosing.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Adrenal crisis (salt-wasting crisis):** hypovolaemic shock + hyponatraemia + hyperkalaemia + hypoglycaemia. *Single finding:* hypotension that does not correct with fluids alone, plus rapid drop in sodium.
- **Severe hyperkalaemia (K+ >6.5 mmol/L):** broad QRS, peaked T waves → cardiac arrest. *Single finding:* ECG showing hyperkalaemia in a shocked neonate or known CAH patient → treat empirically.
- **Missed stress-dose in sick patient:** any infection, surgery, or trauma in a known CAH patient → can precipitate crisis within hours. *Single finding:* known CAH + intercurrent illness + no extra steroid → treat immediately.

### Presentation
- **Neonate (classic salt-wasting):** vomiting, lethargy, poor feeding, dehydration, ambiguous genitalia in females (46,XX), hyperpigmentation (nipples, scrotum). Hyponatraemia, hyperkalaemia, hypoglycaemia.
- **Simple virilising (no salt-wasting):** girls with ambiguous genitalia, boys with phallic enlargement, early pubic hair, accelerated growth.
- **Non-classic (late-onset):** hirsutism, oligomenorrhoea, infertility, acne, precocious puberty in children.
- **Older known CAH (adult):** admitted for surgery, infection, or trauma → missing stress-dose → crisis. Or incidentally found electrolytes.
- **Key exam findings:** look for hyperpigmentation (hands, oral mucosa), signs of virilisation (clitoromegaly, penile growth, early pubic hair), hypotension.

### Differential Diagnosis
- **In neonate with ambiguous genitalia:** true hermaphroditism, partial androgen insensitivity syndrome, 11β-hydroxylase deficiency. *Differentiator:* 17-OHP markedly elevated only in 21-OHD.
- **In older child or adult with virilisation:** polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS), adrenal/ovarian tumour (androgen-secreting). *Differentiator:* 17-OHP + ACTH stimulation test — PCOS does not have raised 17-OHP after ACTH.
- **In adrenal crisis:** septic shock, hypovolaemia from gastroenteritis, other causes of hyponatraemia (SIADH, adrenal insufficiency not CAH), hyperkalaemia (renal failure, hypoaldosteronism).
- **Splitter:** **17-hydroxyprogesterone** — in 21-OHD it is grossly elevated; in all other adrenal insufficiency or sepsis it is normal. Do not rely on random cortisol alone.

### Investigations
- **Bedside:**
  - Capillary blood glucose (hypoglycaemia common in crisis)
  - ECG for hyperkalaemia (peaked T, widened QRS)
  - Urine output (oliguria in shock)
- **Bloods:**
  - U&Es (hyponatraemia, hyperkalaemia), glucose
  - Cortisol (random, but **do not wait**: treat first) — low <100 nmol/L suggests insufficiency
  - ACTH (elevated in primary adrenal insufficiency)
  - **17-hydroxyprogesterone (17-OHP)** — diagnostic for 21-OHD (↑↑)
  - Renin (elevated in salt-wasting form)
  - Blood gas (metabolic acidosis in crisis)
- **Imaging:**
  - **Neonate:** pelvic ultrasound for uterus/ovaries (to assign sex of rearing)
  - **Adrenal ultrasound:** may show enlarged adrenals
  - **Knee or wrist X-ray:** bone age to assess virilisation effect (advanced)
- **Special tests:**
  - **ACTH (Synacthen) stimulation test:** baseline + 30/60-min cortisol and 17-OHP (if diagnosis uncertain). In CAH, cortisol fails to rise; 17-OHP rises exaggerated.
  - Continuous glucose monitoring if hypoglycaemic episodes.
- **Never-miss test:** **17-hydroxyprogesterone** (send urgently if suspected). Also **pre-treatment cortisol** before giving hydrocortisone (but treat immediately if unstable).

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - **Adrenal crisis:** IV access, fluid bolus 20 mL/kg 0.9% saline (repeat if needed), IV hydrocortisone 100 mg (adults) or 50 mg/m² (children) immediately. Correct hypoglycaemia (10% dextrose) if present. Monitor K+ closely — treat hyperkalaemia if ECG changes.
  - **Known CAH + illness:** give "stress dose" hydrocortisone IV (or IM if no access) within 30 minutes of presentation. Do not wait for tests.
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - Continue IV hydrocortisone [check local policy] — typically 50–100 mg 6-hourly or continuous infusion 200 mg/24h (adults). Convert to oral when tolerating oral intake.
  - Start fludrocortisone for salt-wasting (adults: 50–200 mcg daily; children: dose per local protocol).
  - Correct electrolytes — sodium replacement if hyponatraemic, treat hyperkalaemia.
  - Endocrine referral for all new diagnoses.
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - **Maintenance:** oral hydrocortisone (10–20 mg/m²/day in 3 divided doses, typical adult 15–25 mg/day), fludrocortisone (50–200 mcg daily). Monitor growth in children, electrolytes, renin, bone age.
  - **Sick day rules:** double/triple oral hydrocortisone during febrile illness, vomiting, diarrhoea; if unable to take oral → IM/IV hydrocortisone. Provide steroid alert card and MedicAlert.
  - Annual endocrine review in adults (adrenal rest tumours testicular US in men).
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - **IV fluids:** 0.9% saline at maintenance rate, plus replacement for dehydration.
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** standard based on age/mobility — consider increased risk with prolonged illness.
  - **Diet:** normal as tolerated; if NBM → IV hydrocortisone + glucose.
  - **Observations:** NEWS2 4-hourly, hourly if unstable. Capillary glucose pre-meals if hypoglycaemia risk.

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line (crisis):** Hydrocortisone 100 mg IV (adults) / 50 mg/m² IV (children) stat, then 50–100 mg IV 6-hourly or 200 mg/24h continuous infusion. [Check local policy for paediatric dosing.]
- **Maintenance oral:** Hydrocortisone 10–20 mg/m²/day in 3 divided doses (e.g., morning 10 mg, afternoon 5 mg, evening 5 mg for adult). Adjust per clinical response and 17-OHP levels.
- **Mineralocorticoid:** Fludrocortisone 50–200 mcg once daily (adults). Dose adjusted by renin and electrolytes.
- **Common alternative:** Prednisolone (once daily) in adults for poor compliance — dose equivalent to hydrocortisone: prednisolone 5 mg ≈ hydrocortisone 20 mg. Use only under specialist supervision.
- **Key contraindications/interactions:**
  - Hydrocortisone: avoid live vaccines during high-dose therapy. Interacts with insulin and antidiabetics (↑ glucose).
  - Fludrocortisone: caution in hypertension, heart failure.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** No specific dose adjustment needed; monitor fluid balance in renal impairment.
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Omitting stress-dose during illness/surgery** – this is the commonest cause of crisis death. Also: giving oral hydrocortisone to a vomiting patient without parenteral replacement.

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Adrenal crisis (shock, hypoglycaemia, hyperkalaemia – cardiac arrest)
  - Hyperkalaemia-related arrhythmia
  - Hypoglycaemic seizure
- **Chronic:**
  - Short stature (overtreatment with glucocorticoids suppresses growth)
  - Obesity, osteoporosis, diabetes, hypertension (iatrogenic Cushing's from over-replacement)
  - Testicular adrenal rest tumours (TART) in males → infertility, testicular pain
  - Female virilisation / menstrual disturbance / subfertility
  - Osteoporosis (prolonged steroid use)

### Prognosis
- Excellent with lifelong therapy and adherence to sick day rules. Salt-wasting crisis is preventable. Mortality in childhood from crisis is <5% in developed countries. Adult life expectancy normal if good control. TART can cause infertility but is treatable.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Give hydrocortisone first, ask questions later** — any patient with known adrenal insufficiency and illness needs stress-dose. Write a "steroid stress prescription" when they are admitted.
- **Always provide a written sick day rule plan and alert jewellery** for every CAH patient at discharge.
- **In a neonate with ambiguous genitalia and electrolytes derangement, start hydrocortisone + fludrocortisone while awaiting 17-OHP** — do not delay treatment to confirm diagnosis.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Giving glucocorticoids without mineralocorticoid in salt-wasting form** — will not correct sodium; may cause fluid overload.
- **Using a random cortisol to rule out CAH** — a normal cortisol does not exclude 21-OH deficiency; you need 17-OHP.
- **Assuming an adult with known CAH is "stable" on their usual dose during intercurrent illness** — they need stress dose; never leave them on maintenance only.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:**
  - Vomiting, diarrhoea, or unable to take oral steroids >1 dose
  - Fever >38°C not controlled with antipyretics
  - Severe headache, dizziness, syncope, abdominal pain, or confusion
- **Follow-up:**
  - Endocrine clinic within 1 week after a crisis. Ensure they have a steroid card, Glucagon pen (if hypoglycaemia risk), and written sick day rules.

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior / ICU if:**
  - NEWS2 ≥5 or persistent hypotension after two fluid boluses
  - K+ >6.5 mmol/L with ECG changes (broad QRS, loss of P wave)
  - GCS <13 or seizures (likely hypoglycaemia) or refractory hypoglycaemia
  - Lactate >2 mmol/L and rising
  - Any new arrhythmia or respiratory arrest
- **Immediate crash call:** cardiac arrest, suspicion of life-threatening hyperkalaemia, or seizure.
